items.q: Why do astronomers infer the existence of dark matter?
items.correct: Visible matter alone cannot account for galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing.
items.misconception: Dark matter has been directly observed in telescopes.
items.random_wrong: Because the universe looks dark at night.
items.why: Students think dark matter has been seen. It's INFERRED from gravitational effects on visible matter and light - galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone, implying unseen matter contributes most gravity.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: A blazar is an AGN whose jet:
items.random_wrong: Is perpendicular to our line of sight
items.why: Correct answer: Points nearly at Earth. “Is invisible” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's High redshift. “Low redshift” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Seyfert published his AGN paper?
items.why: Exact answer: 1943. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
